questions.q: What was Mahatma Gandhi's full original name?
questions.explanation: Gandhi's full name was Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi.

questions.q: Gandhi is honoured in India by which title meaning 'Father of the Nation'?
questions.explanation: Gandhi is revered as Rashtrapita, the Father of the Nation.

questions.q: Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolence is captured by which term?
questions.explanation: Ahimsa, meaning non-violence, was the moral foundation of Gandhi's political philosophy.

questions.q: Gandhi's method of nonviolent resistance was called what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi developed satyagraha, the technique of nonviolent resistance based on truth and moral force.

questions.q: In which year was Mahatma Gandhi assassinated?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was assassinated on 30 January 1948.

questions.q: Gandhi was born in which present-day Indian state?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was born at Porbandar in present-day Gujarat.

questions.q: The handspun cloth Gandhi promoted as a symbol of self-reliance is called what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi championed khadi, handspun and handwoven cloth, as an emblem of self-reliance.

questions.q: In 1930 Gandhi led a famous march to the sea to protest the tax on which commodity?
questions.explanation: Gandhi led the Dandi Salt March in 1930 to defy the British monopoly and tax on salt.

questions.q: Gandhi's wife and lifelong companion was named what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi's wife was Kasturba Gandhi, affectionately called Ba.

questions.q: Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement in 1942 with which famous call?
questions.explanation: Gandhi gave the call 'Do or Die' at the launch of the Quit India Movement in August 1942.

questions.q: On what date was Gandhi born?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was born on 2 October 1869, a date now observed as Gandhi Jayanti.

questions.q: Gandhi went to which country in 1893, where he first developed satyagraha against racial discrimination?
questions.explanation: Gandhi spent over two decades in South Africa, where he first evolved satyagraha against racial injustice.

questions.q: Gandhi studied law in which city in England?
questions.explanation: Gandhi trained as a barrister in London, qualifying at the Inner Temple.

questions.q: Gandhi regarded which moderate leader as his political guru?
questions.explanation: Gandhi considered Gopal Krishna Gokhale his political mentor and teacher.

questions.q: Gandhi called off the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1922 after violence at which place?
questions.explanation: Gandhi suspended the Non-Cooperation Movement after a mob killed policemen at Chauri Chaura in February 1922.

questions.q: Which two newspapers were closely associated with Gandhi's editorship in India?
options: Amrita Bazar Patrika and Bande Mataram
questions.explanation: Gandhi edited the journals Young India and later Harijan to spread his ideas.

questions.q: Gandhi's first major satyagraha in India, in 1917, took place at which location?
questions.explanation: Gandhi's first major Indian satyagraha was the Champaran campaign in Bihar in 1917.

questions.q: In which South African incident was Gandhi thrown off a train, a turning point in his life?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was ejected from a first-class railway carriage at Pietermaritzburg station, an episode that shaped his resolve to fight injustice.

questions.q: Gandhi signed a pact in 1931 with which Viceroy, suspending civil disobedience?
questions.explanation: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact of March 1931 was concluded with Viceroy Lord Irwin.

questions.q: Gandhi attended which Round Table Conference in London as the sole Congress representative?
questions.explanation: Gandhi attended the Second Round Table Conference in 1931 as the sole representative of the Congress.

questions.q: The ashram Gandhi established near Ahmedabad in 1917, the starting point of the Salt March, was named after which river?
questions.explanation: Gandhi founded the Sabarmati Ashram on the bank of the Sabarmati river near Ahmedabad.

questions.q: Gandhi undertook a 'fast unto death' in 1932 in opposition to which colonial proposal affecting depressed classes?
options: The Communal Award and separate electorates
questions.explanation: Gandhi fasted against the separate electorates for depressed classes in the Communal Award, leading to the Poona Pact.

questions.q: Who assassinated Mahatma Gandhi?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was shot dead by Nathuram Godse on 30 January 1948 in New Delhi.

questions.q: Gandhi's first book, written in 1909, presenting his critique of modern civilization was titled what?
questions.explanation: Gandhi wrote 'Hind Swaraj' (Indian Home Rule) in 1909, outlining his vision of self-rule and critique of Western civilization.

questions.q: In which year did Gandhi return permanently to India from South Africa?
questions.explanation: Gandhi returned to India for good in January 1915 after his long stay in South Africa.

questions.q: Gandhi's autobiography, written in Gujarati, carries which English title?
options: The Story of My Experiments with Truth
questions.explanation: Gandhi's autobiography is titled 'The Story of My Experiments with Truth'.

questions.q: During the Quit India period Gandhi and Kasturba were imprisoned at which palace, where Kasturba died?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was detained at the Aga Khan Palace in Pune during Quit India, where Kasturba died in 1944.

questions.q: Which influential Russian writer corresponded with Gandhi and influenced his ideas on nonviolence?
questions.explanation: Gandhi corresponded with Leo Tolstoy, whose writings on nonviolence deeply influenced him; he named a settlement Tolstoy Farm.

questions.q: In the aftermath of independence and partition, Gandhi was in which city undertaking fasts to quell communal violence in early 1948?
questions.explanation: Gandhi was in Delhi in January 1948, having fasted to promote communal peace, when he was assassinated.

questions.q: Gandhi described a hierarchy of his constructive programme; which of these was a key plank he tirelessly promoted?
questions.explanation: The removal of untouchability was a central plank of Gandhi's constructive programme, alongside khadi and communal harmony.
